title: Documentation and Ticketing Systems
obj: Students will be able to create accurate ticket documentation, navigate a ticketing system, and explain why proper documentation is essential in IT support.
content: Why documentation matters
knowledge transfer, accountability, SLA measurement, compliance, training new staff
Ticketing system components
ticket fields (priority, category, requester, assignee, status), SLA timers, escalation paths
Writing good ticket notes
what the user reported, what was observed, steps taken, outcome; avoiding jargon
Knowledge base articles
converting resolved tickets into reusable KB articles; structure and search tags
Asset and inventory documentation
CMDB concepts; tracking hardware, software, and relationships
Change log and runbooks
documenting recurring procedures as runbooks; change records in ITSM tools
assess: Ticket writing lab: given a scenario description, write a complete ticket from open through close
KB article draft: convert a completed troubleshooting ticket into a reusable KB article
Short answer: why is it important to document steps that did NOT fix the problem?

title: Change Management Procedures
obj: Students will be able to describe the change management process, classify change types, and explain why formal change control reduces IT risk.
content: Why change management
uncontrolled changes cause most outages; change process reduces risk and improves predictability
Change types
standard (pre-approved, low risk), normal (requires CAB review), emergency (expedited for critical fixes)
Change request components
description, risk assessment, rollback plan, testing evidence, implementation window
Change Advisory Board
who reviews changes; CAB meeting frequency; approval criteria
Change window
why changes happen in maintenance windows; communication to stakeholders
Post-change review
verifying success, documenting lessons learned, closing the change record
assess: Change classification: given 8 IT changes, classify each as standard/normal/emergency and explain
Change request draft: write a change request for upgrading a server's OS, including risk and rollback
Short answer: what is a rollback plan and why is it required in every change request?

title: Safety Procedures in IT Work
obj: Students will be able to apply electrical safety practices, handle equipment safely, and identify workplace hazards specific to IT environments.
content: Electrical safety
never work on live equipment; discharge capacitors (monitors/UPS); ESD wrist straps; GFCI outlets
Lifting and ergonomics
proper lifting technique for heavy servers; two-person lifts; server rail systems; ergonomic workstation setup
Toxic materials
toner powder (respiratory risk), CRT phosphor, battery acid, thermal paste; proper PPE
Fire safety
electrical fire suppression (CO2, FM-200, not water); server room fire suppression systems
Cable management
preventing trip hazards; proper cable routing in racks; strain relief
Tools and workspace
using the right tool for the job; keeping workspace organized; MSDS/SDS sheets
assess: Hazard identification: given a server room photo, identify 5 safety hazards
PPE selection: for 5 IT tasks, identify what PPE (if any) is appropriate
Short answer: why should you never use water to extinguish an electrical fire?

title: Environmental Impacts and E-Waste
obj: Students will be able to explain the environmental impact of IT equipment, describe responsible e-waste disposal, and identify energy-efficiency practices.
content: E-waste hazards
lead, mercury, cadmium, beryllium in electronics; leaching into soil/water; why landfill disposal is harmful
Responsible disposal
R2 and e-Stewards certified recyclers; manufacturer take-back programs; donation of working equipment
Data sanitization before disposal
must sanitize before recycling or donating; reference to data-destruction-and-disposal atom
Energy efficiency
80 PLUS power supplies; Energy Star certification; server power management; virtualization reducing physical hardware
Data center environmental controls
PUE metric; cooling efficiency; hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment; renewable energy use
Green IT initiatives
thin clients, device refresh cycles, paper reduction, remote work reducing commute/office energy
assess: E-waste research: find your local certified e-waste recycler and document their accepted items and process
Energy calculation: given a list of 5 computers, calculate annual electricity cost at current kWh rates
Short answer: what hazardous material in older CRT monitors makes them especially dangerous to landfill?

title: Remote Access Technologies
obj: Students will be able to compare remote access technologies, configure a VPN connection, and use remote support tools to assist end users.
content: VPN types
site-to-site (connecting two offices), client-to-site (remote worker); split tunneling
VPN protocols
IPSec/IKEv2, OpenVPN, WireGuard, SSL/TLS VPN; L2TP (legacy); which to recommend today
RDP and SSH
RDP for Windows GUI remote; SSH for Linux/network device CLI; security hardening for each
Remote support tools
TeamViewer, AnyDesk, SCCM Remote Control; attended vs. unattended; session recording
Zero trust network access
replacing VPN for many orgs; identity-aware proxy; least-privilege access per application
Remote access security
MFA requirement, session timeout, logging, geofencing; risks of exposed RDP port
assess: VPN configuration lab: configure a client-to-site VPN on a Windows PC and verify the tunnel
Protocol comparison table: compare IPSec, OpenVPN, and WireGuard on security, speed, and ease of setup
Short answer: why is exposing RDP directly to the internet a significant security risk?

title: Scripting Basics (Batch, Shell, PowerShell)
obj: Students will be able to read and write basic automation scripts in Batch, Bash, and PowerShell to automate repetitive IT tasks.
content: Why scripting
automation saves time; consistency vs. manual steps; auditability; scaling IT operations
Batch scripting
.bat files; variables (%VAR%), IF/ELSE, FOR loops; echo, pause, call; practical uses (mapped drives, cleanup)
Bash scripting
#!/bin/bash shebang; variables ($VAR), if/then/fi, for loops, chmod +x; practical uses (backups, log rotation)
PowerShell scripting
cmdlets, pipeline, $variables, foreach, if; Get-Content/Out-File; execution policy; practical admin scripts
Reading existing scripts
following logic without being the author; identifying what a script does before running it
Script safety
never run untrusted scripts; testing in a VM; commenting code; error handling with try/catch
assess: Script reading: given a 20-line PowerShell script, describe in plain English what it does
Write a Batch script: create a .bat file that maps a network drive and logs the date/time to a text file
Short answer: what does Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned do and why does it matter?

title: AI Concepts and Uses in IT Support
obj: Students will be able to describe foundational AI and machine learning concepts, identify how AI is used in IT support tools, and recognize the limitations of AI-assisted support.
content: AI and ML basics
supervised vs. unsupervised learning; training data; models; AI is pattern recognition, not understanding
AI in IT support tools
AI-assisted ticketing (auto-categorization, suggested resolutions), chatbots, predictive alerting
AI in security
anomaly detection, user behavior analytics (UBA/UEBA), AI-powered antivirus (EDR)
Generative AI in IT
using LLMs for script writing, documentation, knowledge base drafting; prompt engineering basics
Limitations and risks
hallucinations, bias in training data, over-reliance, privacy concerns with sending data to AI
Responsible AI use
verifying AI-generated scripts before running, not sending PII to public AI tools, human oversight
assess: AI tool survey: use an AI assistant to generate a PowerShell script, then audit it for correctness and safety
Limitation analysis: given 3 AI-generated IT recommendations, identify any errors or risks
Short answer: why should you never paste customer PII into a public AI chat tool?

title: System Administration Roles and Responsibilities
obj: Students will be able to describe the responsibilities of a system administrator and distinguish the sysadmin role from help desk, network, and security roles.
content: Sysadmin scope
managing servers, OS, user accounts, software deployment, backups, monitoring — the full stack
Sysadmin vs. help desk
help desk handles end-user incidents; sysadmin handles infrastructure and escalated issues
Common sysadmin tasks
provisioning/deprovisioning accounts, patching servers, capacity planning, on-call response
Infrastructure documentation
network diagrams, runbooks, change logs, asset inventory — sysadmin as documentation owner
Automation in sysadmin work
scripting repetitive tasks; configuration management (Ansible, Group Policy); why automation reduces error
Career path
help desk → junior sysadmin → senior sysadmin → infrastructure architect; certifications (MCSA, RHCSA, VCP)
assess: Role comparison: create a table distinguishing help desk, sysadmin, network admin, and security analyst by daily tasks
Scenario triage: given 10 IT incidents, classify each as help desk vs. sysadmin responsibility
Short answer: why is documentation considered one of the most important sysadmin skills?

title: Active Directory and Group Policy
obj: Students will be able to navigate Active Directory to manage users, groups, and OUs, and explain how Group Policy applies settings across a domain.
content: Active Directory concepts
domain, forest, tree; domain controller; AD as a centralized directory for authentication and authorization
AD objects
users, groups (security vs. distribution), computers, OUs (organizational units); CN, DN notation
User and group management
creating users, setting attributes, adding to groups, resetting passwords, disabling accounts in ADUC
Group Policy Objects
GPO scope: site, domain, OU; policy inheritance; blocking inheritance; enforcing GPOs; RSOP
Common GPO use cases
password policy, screen lock, software deployment, drive mapping, desktop wallpaper, restricting Control Panel
LDAP and AD queries
what LDAP is; using AD search (dsquery, PowerShell Get-ADUser); filtering by attribute
assess: AD lab: create an OU, create 3 user accounts, add them to a security group, and apply a GPO to the OU
GPO troubleshoot: use gpresult /r to determine which policies are applying to a user
Short answer: a new employee can log in but cannot access shared drives — where in AD do you look first?
